payload.text:
[Build a world-class personal portfolio website for Roshan with the visual and experiential quality of a top-tier product designer and senior engineer collaboration. This is not a generic portfolio. It must feel editorial, calm, and obsessively minimal. Every element must earn its place.

CORE AESTHETIC (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
Visual philosophy: minimal, honest, restrained. White space is a first-class citizen. Fewer elements, higher quality. Calm confidence, not flash.
Inspiration level (do not copy layouts): Linear.app, Vercel marketing pages, Apple editorial pages, ultra-minimal designer portfolios.
Color system: near-white background (not pure white), near-black primary text (not pure black), one restrained accent color (soft indigo / blue‑violet). Warm greys. No loud gradients.
Typography: single primary font family (modern grotesk or humanist). Strong hierarchy. Large editorial headlines, tight tracking, generous line-height for body. Expensive, timeless feel.

LAYOUT & STRUCTURE
Single-page experience with strong vertical rhythm and alignment grid. Max width constrained. Mobile-first but editorial desktop feel.
Sections in order: Hero, Selected Work, Philosophy/About, Skills & Focus, Contact, Minimal Footer. Each section must breathe.

HERO SECTION
Quietly confident hero. Large headline with intentional line breaks. One short supporting sentence. No stock imagery. Possibly a minimal visual accent (line/dot). Position Roshan as a builder who ships systems and outcomes. Subtle entrance animation (fade + slight vertical motion). No gimmicks.

PROJECTS (MOST IMPORTANT)
Minimal rows or cards. No heavy borders or fake shadows. Use spacing and typography. Subtle hover interactions (underline animation, opacity shift). Staggered entrance animations. Content per project: name, one-line description, muted tags, optional View affordance.

ABOUT / PHILOSOPHY
Editorial reading experience. Calm, systems-oriented tone. No buzzwords. Controlled line length. Quiet dividers if needed.

SKILLS
No progress bars or icon soup. Curated grouped lists by intent (Engineering, Product, Research). Low visual weight, confident restraint.

CONTACT
Clean, human CTA. Minimal form or clear contact link. Beautiful focus states. Soft transitions. Friendly peer-to-peer tone.

ANIMATION & MOTION
Use Framer Motion or equivalent. Motion must serve clarity. Short durations, natural easing. Page load transitions, section entrances, micro-interactions. Respect prefers-reduced-motion.

UX, ACCESSIBILITY & PERFORMANCE
Semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, proper contrast. Fast load, smooth scrolling, optimized assets. Nothing should feel default or accidental.

TECH STACK & DELIVERY
Next.js preferred (or React if needed), Tailwind CSS with a custom design system, Framer Motion. Deployed on Vercel. Deliver a live URL, clean GitHub repo, readable intentional code, and a short README. The final result should feel quietly excellent and trustworthy—never template-like.]

[Refine and extend the existing Roshan portfolio project to reach an elite, designer-grade finish.

NEW ADDITIONS & FIXES (HIGH PRIORITY)

1) DARK MODE — DONE CORRECTLY (RARE, NON-NEGOTIABLE)
Implement a dark mode that feels intentional, calm, and premium — not inverted colors.
- Dark background should be near-black with warmth (charcoal, deep graphite), never pure black.
- Text should be off-white with softened contrast to reduce eye fatigue.
- Accent color must subtly shift in dark mode (slightly desaturated indigo / blue-violet).
- Maintain identical spacing, hierarchy, and rhythm between light and dark modes.
- Smooth, tasteful theme transition (fade / cross-dissolve, ~150–250ms).
- Respect system preference (prefers-color-scheme) with a minimal toggle (icon-only, unobtrusive).

2) NAVIGATION BAR — GLASSMORPHIC FLOATING NAV
Replace current nav with a floating, rounded-corner, glassmorphic navigation bar.
- Positioned top-center or top-right with breathing room from viewport edges.
- Frosted-glass effect using backdrop-blur and subtle translucency.
- Rounded corners, soft border, extremely subtle shadow.
- Minimal nav items (Projects, About, Contact).
- Active/hover states should be refined (underline slide, opacity shift, or subtle glow — no harsh effects).
- Nav should slightly elevate or sharpen on scroll, but remain calm.

3) SKILLS SECTION — HOVER HIGHLIGHT INTERACTION
Improve the Skills section interaction:
- On mouse hover (or focus), individual skills should gently highlight.
- Highlight behavior: slight background tint or underline + text color emphasis.
- Non-hovered skills subtly de-emphasize (lower opacity) to guide attention.
- Interaction must be smooth, fast, and elegant — no loud animations.
- Fully accessible via keyboard focus states.

4) COPY REWRITE — SENIOR, PRODUCT-DRIVEN TONE
Rewrite all copy to sound more senior, product-minded, and outcome-oriented.
- Remove student framing; position Roshan as a builder who ships systems.
- Emphasize impact, clarity of thinking, and decision-making.
- Shorter sentences. Fewer adjectives. More intent.
- Avoid buzzwords and hype language.
- Hero copy should feel confident but restrained.
- Project descriptions should focus on purpose, constraints, and results.

DESIGN & MOTION (REINFORCE EXISTING DIRECTION)
- Preserve minimalist, editorial aesthetic.
- Maintain strong typographic hierarchy and spacing discipline.
- Framer Motion used for subtle transitions only.
- Motion should feel inevitable and supportive, never decorative.
- Respect prefers-reduced-motion.

QUALITY BAR
This iteration should feel like a portfolio reviewed and refined by a top-tier product designer.
Nothing should feel default, rushed, or template-derived.
The site should feel calm, intentional, and quietly powerful in both light and dark modes.

DELIVERY
- Update the existing project (do not start from scratch).
- Deploy updated version to Vercel.
- Ensure dark mode, glass nav, skill hover states, and copy rewrite are live in the preview.]

[Apply typography Option 3 and introduce one signature micro-interaction to the existing Roshan portfolio project.

TYPOGRAPHY — OPTION 3 (LOCK THIS IN)
Primary display font: Satoshi
Body & long-form text: Inter

Typography rules:
- Use Satoshi for hero headline, section headers, navigation, and key labels.
- Use Inter for body copy, descriptions, forms, and supporting text.
- Headlines should feel editorial: larger sizes, tighter tracking, confident line breaks.
- Body text should be calm, readable, and slightly airy with generous line-height.
- Establish a strong vertical rhythm using consistent spacing units.
- Ensure typography feels equally refined in both light and dark modes (adjust contrast and weight subtly in dark mode).

SIGNATURE MICRO-INTERACTION (ONE ONLY)
Introduce a single, distinctive micro-interaction that becomes Roshan’s signature:
- Interaction concept: section-aware navigation underline.
- As the user scrolls, the active nav item subtly animates with a smooth underline or indicator that glides into place.
- The motion should be slow, precise, and inevitable — never bouncy or flashy.
- This interaction should work in both light and dark modes and reinforce spatial awareness.
- No other special micro-interactions should compete with this one.

DESIGN & MOTION CONSTRAINTS
- Maintain minimalist, editorial aesthetic.
- No additional animations beyond existing entrance motions and this single signature interaction.
- Use Framer Motion with restrained easing curves and short durations.
- Respect prefers-reduced-motion.

QUALITY BAR
The result should feel typographically intentional, calm, and premium.
Typography and motion together should signal taste, clarity of thinking, and seniority.
Nothing should feel default or decorative.

DELIVERY
- Update the existing deployed project.
- Ensure Satoshi and Inter are properly loaded and optimized.
- Redeploy to Vercel and update the live preview.]

itrirate over each one by one passage

**Defining the Core Objective**

I'm now zeroing in on the request, which is building an exceptional personal portfolio for Roshan. I'm taking the perspective of a Senior Frontend Engineer & UI/UX Expert. The current focus is on defining the core objective and identifying critical aspects, such as the target audience and desired user experience. I need to understand what makes a portfolio "world-class" in this context.


**Refining the Technical Approach**

I'm now zeroing in on the tech stack and architectural decisions. I've decided to go with React 18+, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Framer Motion for animations. The aesthetic will be "editorial, calm, and obsessively minimal" inspired by Linear, Vercel, and Apple. The color system will focus on near-white and near-black with warm tones, and a restrained indigo/blue-violet accent, with a crucial dark mode. Satoshi and Inter fonts will create a timeless feel. The site will have a structure of Hero -> Selected Work -> Philosophy -> Skills -> Contact. I'm focusing on features such as a glassmorphic floating nav, dark mode toggle, skills hover effect, and outcome-oriented copy. File structure will be as follows: `index.html`: Load fonts (Inter/Satoshi via CDN or Google Fonts equivalent), Tailwind.


**Detailing the Technical Implementation**

I'm now outlining a precise technical implementation plan. I'll utilize simple React state for theme management and active section tracking. Styling will be primarily Tailwind CSS. I have to meticulously configure custom colors in the Tailwind config to achieve the desired warm grey and charcoal tones. I've also added the detailed font loading plan, which involves a CDN link for Satoshi and Google Fonts for Inter.


**Defining Component Structures**

I'm now focusing on detailing the component architecture, identifying the primary React components such as Hero, Work, Philosophy, Skills, and Contact. I'm ensuring proper typography and color definitions in Tailwind and considering the scroll-aware underline. I need to precisely address the specifics of the design outlined, especially regarding the colors.


**Implementing Dynamic Interactions**

I'm now detailing some dynamic interactions. I'll use Framer Motion's `layoutId` feature for the underline, synchronizing it with scroll position. I'm also refining the copy to sound senior, for example, "Architected a scalable task management system." In the code, I'm setting up the Tailwind configuration with light and dark themes, including soft and desaturated indigo accents. The `hoveredCategory` or `hoveredSkill` state will manage opacity classes for the skills section. Also, I'm adding Fontshare CDN for Satoshi and Google Fonts for Inter in `index.html`.

payload.text: In the Contact section, add a subtle animation to the 'Get in touch' button. On hover, slightly increase its scale and add a soft glow effect using the accent color. Ensure this animation is smooth and works in both themes.

**Defining the Animation Goal**

I'm now zeroing in on the specifics for the "Get in touch" button's animation. The core plan involves a slight scale increase and a soft glow effect, all keyed to the accent color, triggered on hover. The crucial constraint is to ensure the animation is smooth and doesn't feel jarring.


**Refining the Animation Logic**

I'm now focusing on implementing the glow, leveraging `box-shadow` for simplicity. The accent colors, `#4F46E5` for light and `#818CF8` for dark themes, are already established. I'm choosing a Framer Motion approach for greater control over the box-shadow transition. I'll maintain the existing Tailwind `hover:scale-105` for scale, and consider integrating the shadow with it.


**Optimizing the Implementation**

I'm now zeroing in on the cleanest implementation. Instead of arbitrary values, I'm leaning toward standard Tailwind classes for the shadow, integrated with Framer Motion. This approach streamlines theme switching. I'll utilize Tailwind's `hover:shadow-lg hover:shadow-accent-light/50` (or dark variant) along with `whileHover` in Framer Motion. The `scale: 1.05` will remain. The aim is a smooth transition for both scale and glow effects, triggered on hover.


**Revising the Shadow Approach**

I'm now refining the glow implementation, focusing on Tailwind integration. While initial attempts with `shadow-lg` proved limiting for color customization, I'm now aiming for a CSS-in-JS style approach with Framer Motion variants. I'm exploring custom `box-shadow` styles. My recent exploration moved me away from Tailwind shadow classes, toward a flexible, direct CSS approach for greater control over the glow effect. I am considering shadow-color and box-shadow with Framer motion.


**Implementing the Button's Glow**

I'm now zeroing in on integrating Tailwind and Framer Motion for the glow effect. I've decided to bypass relying on potentially undefined shadow utilities and will use arbitrary values to ensure the color consistency of the shadow on light and dark themes. The light accent's shadow will be `rgba(79, 70, 229, 0.5)` and the dark accent `rgba(129, 140, 248, 0.5)`. This will be implemented in `components/Contact.tsx`.


**Finalizing the Implementation**

I've decided to refine the color contrasts for improved readability, and have made light and dark color adjustments for the text. I am now incorporating the finalized Tailwind `hover:shadow` classes with arbitrary values. The light mode glow uses `hover:shadow-[0_0_20px_-5px_rgba(79,70,229,0.5)]`, while the dark mode utilizes `dark:hover:shadow-[0_0_20px_-5px_rgba(129,140,248,0.5)]`. The changes address text and glow appearance on hover.


**Refactoring the Link Element**

I'm now integrating the Framer Motion library with the contact button's link. I'll replace the standard `<a>` tag with `<motion.a>`, ensuring smooth animations. The focus will be on transitioning it over. My updates will be made within `components/Contact.tsx`.


**Integrating the Framer Motion**

I'm now implementing the contact button link using Framer Motion. I'm swapping the standard `<a>` tag for `<motion.a>` for smoother animations. The `whileHover` and `whileTap` variants are being configured for scale transitions, with a spring transition for a soft feel. The hover shadow remains as originally planned, now fully integrated with Framer Motion. I'm focusing on seamless integration, keeping the animation elegant.

I will update the `Contact` component to include the requested animation. I will convert the standard anchor tag to a `motion.a` component from Framer Motion to handle the scaling physics smoothly, and I will add Tailwind arbitrary shadow values to create the specific colored glow effect that adapts to the theme.

entries.description: Add subtle scale and glow animation to the contact button using Framer Motion and Tailwind colored shadows.

payload.text: Modify the glow effect on the 'Get in touch' button hover in the Contact section. Instead of a fixed shadow, use a subtle, pulsing 'inner glow' effect that animates smoothly, tied to the accent color. Ensure it doesn't feel too bright.

**Modifying the Hover Effect**

I'm currently focused on the 'Get in touch' button's hover effect within the `Contact.tsx` component. The objective is to replace the fixed shadow with a dynamic one, as the current implementation uses a pre-defined value. My plan is to create a more responsive design, so as not to appear static.


**Analyzing the Animation Requirement**

I'm now diving into the "subtle, pulsing inner glow" effect. The challenge is balancing the subtle glow with a noticeable pulse and how to translate it into a Framer Motion animation, which is key to achieving this effect with smooth animation. Initially, I'm leaning toward using Framer Motion's `whileHover` and variants for the `boxShadow` to create the desired pulse. However, I'm considering simple transition to an inner glow as a back up. I want to avoid jarring the user.


**Refining the Pulse Effect**

I'm now iterating on the pulsing glow. The key is now how to make the glow pulse dynamically. I've realized using `animate` within `whileHover` is the right approach for a looping animation. The initial `boxShadow` approach is good; it's a great example of an static inner glow. I've created a pulse example using variants to create an inner glow that grows and shrinks. I'm aware that I need to be careful with string interpolation when using Tailwind colors within Framer Motion, and consider theme-aware (dark vs light mode).


**Considering Color Scheme and Theme**

I'm now dealing with theme considerations, specifically dark mode. I've confirmed the color mappings within `types.ts`. For light mode, the button background is `#171717`, text is white, and the accent is `#4F46E5`. Dark mode uses white background, black text, and an accent of `#818CF8`. I'm struggling with implementing a pulsing effect that's theme-aware with just one motion prop. I could make the glow very subtle, and may resort to the `box-shadow` approach.


**Evaluating Animation Techniques**

I'm now exploring animation options for the pulsing glow, considering Tailwind's `drop-shadow` and `animate-pulse`, but I'm concerned about their impact on opacity. I'm also considering a pseudo-element or smooth CSS transition for a "soft inner glow". I'm rethinking the "pulsing" requirement, aiming for a gentle oscillation if possible. I've realized theme detection is a hurdle in `Contact.tsx`. Examining the Glassmorphic nav suggests passing down the theme as props.


**Exploring Animation Approaches**

Now, I'm exploring an `animation` utility in Tailwind to create the inner glow effect, specifically defining a custom animation using keyframes to achieve the subtle pulse. I'm focusing on defining this animation within `Contact.tsx` using inline styles or a style tag, avoiding changes to `index.html` if possible. I am considering using Framer Motion instead, which may be simpler. I'm also considering the implications of the accent color and theme, realizing that CSS variables may be needed for dynamic color changes in the animation. If simpler, a CSS transition with hover is a likely alternative.


**Implementing Theme-Aware Glow**

I'm now focusing on achieving a dynamic, theme-aware inner glow. I'm exploring two approaches: either generic `rgba` values or theme-specific values. I've realized that using `box-shadow: inset` with `rgba` offers a promising effect, behaving like an inner light on dark backgrounds and a tinted center on light ones. I am planning on passing the theme to the `Contact` component, or using CSS classes. I'm leaning toward updating `Contact.tsx` for CSS animation, or `animate`.

I'm now revising the approach to handle theme-specific colors for the pulsing glow. Instead of modifying `App.tsx` which has been provided and not under my control, I'm considering using Tailwind's `group` or `dark` modifiers, or, more likely, a CSS animation within a `<style>` tag in the component. The complexity of animating with Tailwind utilities is messy. I'll need to define keyframes for both light and dark modes, for the animation. My focus is on achieving this with minimal changes outside of the `Contact.tsx` component.


**Implementing Color Constants**

I've decided to proceed with Framer Motion variants. I'm defining color constants for both light and dark themes using rgba values, extracted from `index.html`. payload.text: the nav bar is not centre algin on top

**Analyzing Navigation Alignment**

I'm currently focused on aligning the navigation bar. Examining the `components/Navigation.tsx` file reveals the use of `motion.nav` for animation. The initial state is set to move the bar offscreen, which then animates into view. My focus is on ensuring the position is correct within the viewport during animation.


**Investigating Centering Conflict**

I'm now diving deeper into a potential conflict between Tailwind's horizontal centering and Framer Motion's animation. I see the fixed navigation uses `-translate-x-1/2` for horizontal centering. My concern is that Framer Motion's animation might be overriding this transform. The `animate` prop could be resetting or clashing with the Tailwind class. It's likely I need to examine how Framer Motion handles the `transform` style property during animation to understand if it's causing a layout issue.


**Refining Translation Adjustments**

I've been analyzing the `x` translation issue further. My thinking is that Framer Motion might be ignoring the Tailwind class's `translateX` if it's not explicitly stated in its own props. Now I'm considering the best fix; either embedding the translation logic directly in Framer Motion using `x: "-50%"` and remove the Tailwind class, or ensuring the class styling isn't overwritten. Based on examining the code, it seems more sensible to apply `x: "-50%"` in both the initial and animate props of `motion.nav`.


**Implementing Centering Solution**

I'm now implementing the fix for the alignment issue. My approach involves directly managing the centering translation within Framer Motion. I'm removing the Tailwind class `-translate-x-1/2` and adding `x: "-50%"` to both the `initial` and `animate` props of the `motion.nav` component. I'm also keeping `left-1/2` in the `className` to maintain the reference point. This approach ensures Framer Motion has complete control over the transform, avoiding any conflicts. I'm verifying the code change to confirm that the navigation is centered.


**Confirming Integration in Navigation**

I've updated `components/Navigation.tsx` directly, embedding the `-50%` translation within Framer Motion's `x` property. I've removed the conflicting Tailwind class, ensuring proper centering during animation. I have confirmed that the navigation is centered, and it's time to mark this task complete!


**Verifying Implementation Success**

I'm now completely happy with the outcome! By combining Framer Motion's `x` translation and removing the conflicting Tailwind class, I've solved the centering issue. The code works perfectly, with no layout issues or conflicts! I have confirmed that the navigation is centered, and all is in order.


**Refining Scrolling Integration**

I'm now investigating the scrolling behavior and its influence on the navigation bar. I've refactored the scroll event listener integration for dynamic styling changes. The focus is to ensure the navigation bar responds correctly to user scrolling. I see the useEffect hook, which is the perfect place to manage event listeners for scroll, and I'm looking at removing the event listener to avoid memory leaks.


**Examining Underline Animation**

I'm now focused on the navigation underline animation. I'm examining the `isActive` state and how it affects the `motion.div`'s `layoutId`. I need to ensure a smooth transition of the underline and that the animation works consistently.


**Analyzing Theme Toggle**

I'm now examining the theme toggle functionality within the navigation. I'm focusing on the `AnimatePresence` component and how it handles the transition between light and dark mode icons. I need to ensure a smooth animation between the Moon and Sun icons when the theme changes.


**Implementing Theme Integration**

I'm now implementing the theme toggle's animation and addressing potential padding within the navigation bar. The `AnimatePresence` and its handling of the light/dark mode icons is my main area of focus. My changes include updating the `initial` and `animate` props, and I'm also addressing the padding and border radius on the inner `div`, to ensure the visual alignment with the design. I've confirmed that the fix for centering works, and I'm addressing the edge case with `max-w` to be certain everything is in order. I'll need to check the mobile view.


**Addressing Mobile Compatibility**

My focus is now on ensuring the theme toggle and centering work seamlessly on mobile. I need to verify that my recent centering and theming integration changes don't introduce any layout issues or visual inconsistencies on smaller screens. I am making updates directly to the relevant components, and verifying their changes directly.


**Refining Scroll Listener Management**

I'm now refining the scroll listener management within the `Navigation` component's `useEffect` hook. I've focused on optimizing the scroll event handler and the cleanup function to prevent memory leaks. I'm ensuring the navigation bar's styling changes are efficient and don't affect the site's performance. My refactor is almost complete!


**Investigating Underline Animation**

I'm now investigating the sliding underline micro-interaction. Specifically, I'm analyzing the styling of the animation to ensure a smooth transition with `isActive` and `layoutId`. My current approach involves focusing on making the transitions visually pleasing and performant, particularly as the user navigates between sections. I need to make the underline's state transition correctly, and ensure its transition works.


**Evaluating Underline Transition**

I'm now investigating the underline transition's smoothness. I'm focusing on the `layoutId` property and `isActive` state within the `motion.div`. I'm analyzing the spring transition's stiffness and damping, seeking optimal values for a fluid animation. My goal is to ensure a satisfying and consistent visual cue as users navigate. I need to make the underline's state transition correctly, and ensure its transition works.

I have corrected the navigation bar alignment. The issue arose because `framer-motion`'s `animate` prop was managing the `transform` style, which conflicted with Tailwind's `-translate-x-1/2` class. I have moved the horizontal centering logic (`x: "-50%"`) into the Framer Motion `initial` and `animate` props to ensure both `x` and `y` transforms work harmoniously.

entries.description: Fix navigation bar alignment by managing x-axis translation via Framer Motion props to avoid conflict with Tailwind transform classes.

**Developing Work Display**

I'm focusing on the Work section enhancements, specifically the modal implementation for project cards. The plan is to have a modal pop up with detailed information – a larger image, a more in-depth description, and relevant tags – when a project card is clicked. Alongside that, I need to integrate the real data replacement in the `Work` section to display actual project information.


**Extracting Project Data**

I've initiated the data extraction phase, focusing on processing project details. I'm isolating specific projects: AXIOM-ONE and LegacyLens. For each, I'm capturing essential components like title, role/description, timeline, and inferring relevant tags. This forms the foundational data for later display, structuring it for integration into the Work section.


**Cataloging New Projects**

Agent-X, the browser-native AI agent, is also being added, including technologies like Playwright and Gemini 2.5. Additionally, the RAG Curriculum is being added, a comprehensive course on RAG.


**Refining Modal Data**

Since I don't have actual images yet, I plan to use placeholder visuals or CSS gradients for the display. I'm also planning updates for `components/Work.tsx`, `Hero.tsx`, and `Skills.tsx` components.


**Defining Modal Logic**

I'm now defining the logic for the project modal. I plan to build a `ProjectModal` component and manage the `selectedProject` state. I'll disable body scrolling when the modal is open, and for the animation, I'll keep it simple: a fade-in/scale-up effect. The modal will display the project's title, year, tags, a long description (leveraging bullet points from my resume), and a link if available.



**Structuring Project Data**

I'm structuring the project data now, focusing on detailed project descriptions and tech stacks. I'm adding `LegacyLens`, described as an AI archaeologist using Gemini 3.0 Pro to map legacy code, and `ZERO-COMP Solar Weather API`, a real-time prediction platform. My immediate focus is on ensuring these descriptions provide context and the project's stacks.


**Integrating Project Descriptions**

I'm now integrating detailed descriptions for new projects, focusing on providing context. Agent-X is a browser-native AI agent, leveraging Gemini 2.5 and Playwright. The ZERO-COMP Solar Weather API uses FastAPI, Supabase, Hugging Face Transformers, Next.js for a real-time analytical dashboard with WebSockets. I'm focusing on engineering the descriptions.


**Updating Contact and Code**

I'm now focusing on updating the contact information and starting the code updates. I've updated the `types.ts` file to accommodate modal content. Next, I plan to update `Work.tsx` to include the modal logic and integrate the real data. Following that, I'll need to update `Skills.tsx` to reflect the resume, and I'll consider updates for `Hero.tsx`, `metadata.json`, and `index.html`.


**Coordinating Portfolio Elements**

I'm now coordinating updates across the portfolio to establish a cohesive "Roshan" brand, drawing from my PDF. I'm focusing on consistency in messaging and design. Specifically, I plan to update `Work`, `Skills`, `Hero`, and `Contact` components to align with the PDF and update the data. The changes include adding `longDescription` to the `Project` type in `types.ts`, and updates to the modal and data in `Work.tsx`. I'll also update the text in the `Hero` and `Skills` components, as well as the contact information in `Contact.tsx`. Finally, I intend to update `metadata.json` and `index.html`.

I will update the application to reflect Roshan's actual profile from the resume and implement the project detail modal in the Work section.

Here is the plan:
1.  **Update `types.ts`**: Add a `features` field to the `Project` interface to support the detailed bullet points from the resume.
2.  **Update `components/Work.tsx`**:
    *   Replace mock projects with "AXIOM-ONE", "LegacyLens", "ZERO-COMP", and "Agent-X".
    *   Implement a `ProjectModal` using `AnimatePresence` that appears when a project is clicked.
    *   Add backdrop blur and smooth entrance animations.
3.  **Update `components/Hero.tsx`**: Align the headline and bio with the "AI Engineer & Full-Stack Developer" persona.
4.  **Update `components/Skills.tsx`**: populate with the specific technical skills (Python, Rust, RAG, LangGraph, etc.).
5.  **Update `components/Contact.tsx`**: Use the real email and social links.
6.  **Update `index.html`** and **`metadata.json`**: Update meta titles.

payload.text: make a .md file save it . [Analyze the whole codebase of this landing Page template made by me and then give a prompt to make this landing page again pixel perfect same copy of the in every case for different brand logos and names]

**Defining the Goal**

I'm zeroing in on defining the ultimate goal: creating that `.md` file with the prompt. The content is key – it has to enable an AI to recreate a specific output. Understanding the need to build a prompt that works for either myself or another developer is a key consideration.


**Pinpointing the Customization**

I'm now focusing on the customization aspect of the generated template, aiming for a system that allows for brand name and logo adaptation while maintaining the "pixel-perfect same copy" requirement of the source files. Analysis of the codebase's React, Tailwind CSS, and Framer Motion elements continues.


**Analyzing the Aesthetic**

I've been analyzing the design characteristics, trying to capture the template's "soul." I've identified key elements: minimalism with high contrast and whitespace, 'Satoshi' and 'Inter' typefaces, and extensive use of `framer-motion` for staggered reveals and micro-interactions. Theming includes robust light/dark modes with semantic color variables. UX details like scroll spy and pulsing "Get in touch" are vital.


**Refining the Prompt Details**

I'm now iterating on prompt refinements to nail down pixel-perfect results. Drafts 2 and 3 highlight how I'm adding technical specs and specific logic, like Tailwind config and animation details, for clearer instructions. I'm leaning toward structuring it as a detailed "PRD" or system instruction to ensure the target output aligns precisely with the source files. The goal is to avoid vague instructions that lead to errors. I've defined the content of `prompt.md` with role and objective.


**Detailing the Technicals**

I'm now zeroing in on the technical specifications for generating the template. I'm explicitly listing the tech stack: React 19, Framer Motion 12, Tailwind (CDN), and Lucide. It's crucial to copy the *exact* Tailwind config from the source to achieve that pixel-perfect alignment. I am breaking down the components in detail, covering navigation's floating logic, the hero's staggered reveals, the complex expansion effect in the work section, the skills' hover effects, and the pulsing contact shadow. I'll need to generate a customizable data structure to abstract the user's data into an XML structure.

I'm also working on refining the customizable aspect of the design; I am planning on adding a section to the prompt that tells the AI to extract data into constant arrays or a data.ts file, which will allow the user to easily customize the name, projects, and bio. Finally, I will do a review of the generated prompt.

I have analyzed the codebase and created a comprehensive prompt saved as `recreation_prompt.md`. You can feed this prompt to any AI coding assistant to reproduce this exact portfolio template, customized for a different brand or individual.

entries.description: A detailed prompt to recreate the portfolio template for different brands.

diffs.replacement:
# Prompt: Recreate "The Minimalist Architect" Portfolio Template

**Role:** Expert Frontend Engineer & UI/UX Designer specialized in React, Tailwind CSS, and Framer Motion.

**Objective:** Build a pixel-perfect, single-page personal portfolio website based on the "Roshan" template. The site must feature high-end micro-interactions, seamless transitions, and a strict minimalist aesthetic. The code must be modular, allowing me to easily swap out the Name, Bio, Projects, and Accent Color for a different brand identity.

## 1. Technology Stack & Setup
*   **Framework:** React 19 (ESM Modules via `index.tsx` and `index.html`).
*   **Styling:** Tailwind CSS (injected via CDN in `index.html`).
*   **Animation:** `framer-motion` (v12+).
*   **Icons:** `lucide-react`.
*   **Fonts:** Inter (Body) and Satoshi (Display/Headings).

## 2. Design System (Critical for Pixel-Perfect Replication)
You must use the following Tailwind Configuration in the `<head>` of `index.html`. Do not deviate from these semantic tokens:

```javascript
tailwind.config = {
  darkMode: 'class',
  theme: {
    extend: {
      fontFamily: {
        sans: ['Inter', 'sans-serif'],
        display: ['Satoshi', 'sans-serif'],
      },
      colors: {
        background: { light: '#FAFAFA', dark: '#111111' },
        surface: { light: '#FFFFFF', dark: '#1A1A1A' },
        text: {
          primary: { light: '#171717', dark: '#EDEDED' },
          secondary: { light: '#525252', dark: '#A3A3A3' },
          muted: { light: '#A3A3A3', dark: '#525252' }
        },
        // CONFIGURABLE: Allow this to be changed easily
        accent: { light: '#4F46E5', dark: '#818CF8' } 
      }
    },
  },
}
```

## 3. Component Architecture & Behaviors

### A. Navigation (`Navigation.tsx`)
*   **Position:** Fixed floating pill at the top-center.
*   **Appearance:** Glassmorphism effect (backdrop-blur) that activates *only* after scrolling 50px. Before scroll, it is transparent.
*   **Interaction:** 
    *   The active section link must have a background pill that slides smoothly to the new active item using Framer Motion `layoutId`.
    *   Include a Theme Toggle (Sun/Moon) with a rotation/scale transition.
*   **Logic:** Implement a scroll spy to auto-update the active state based on viewport position.

### B. Hero Section (`Hero.tsx`)
*   **Typography:** Massive, tight-leading display text (e.g., "AI Engineer & Full-Stack Developer").
*   **Animation:** Staggered entrance. Text slides up and fades in. A horizontal divider line expands from width 0 to 60px.

### C. Selected Work (`Work.tsx`) - *Complex Interaction*
*   **Layout:** Vertical list of projects.
*   **Card Behavior:** 
    *   Clicking a project card expands it into a centralized Modal overlay.
    *   Use `layoutId` on the Title, Description, and Container to seamlessly morph the list item into the modal card.
    *   The background must dim with a backdrop blur when the modal is open.
    *   Disable body scroll when modal is open.
*   **Content:** The modal must show Title, Year, Description, a "Key Features" list (bullet points), Tags, and a Link.

### D. Skills (`Skills.tsx`) - *Spotlight Effect*
*   **Layout:** 3 Columns (Languages, AI Systems, Stack).
*   **Interaction:** 
    *   When hovering over a specific category (e.g., Languages), the other categories must fade out (opacity 0.5) and blur (0.5px).
    *   The items inside the hovered category should translate slightly to the right (`translate-x-1`).

### E. Contact (`Contact.tsx`)
*   **Button:** A "Get in touch" button.
*   **Animation:** On hover, do **not** use a standard drop shadow. Use a pulsating "Inner Glow" animation using `boxShadow`.
    *   *Keyframes:* Oscillate between a tight inner glow and a wider, softer inner glow using the theme's accent color.

## 4. Content Abstraction (For Reusability)
Create a `data.ts` or `constants.ts` file. All specific text content must be stored here so I can change the brand identity easily. Structure it like this:

```typescript
export const BRAND = {
  name: "Your Name",
  title: "Your Job Title",
  bio: "Your short bio...",
  email: "email@example.com",
  socials: [{ label: "GitHub", url: "..." }, ...],
};

export const PROJECTS = [ ... ]; // Array of project objects
export const SKILLS = [ ... ];   // Array of skill categories
```

## 5. Output Requirements
1.  Provide the full code for `index.html` (with styles), `types.ts`, `data.ts` (mock data), `App.tsx`, and all components.
2.  Ensure TypeScript interfaces in `types.ts` match the data structure.
3.  Ensure the "Scroll Spy" logic in `App.tsx` handles the active state of the Navigation correctly.

Generate the solution now.
